text:
UGANDA CHRISTIAN UNIVERSITY 
DEPARTMENT OF COMPUTING 
BACHELOR OF SCIENCE IN COMPUTER SCIENCE / DATA SCIENCE 

COURSE: NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING 
ASSIGNMENT: TEXT ANALYTICS, MORPHOLOGY AND LANGUAGE MODELING 
Semester: ____________________ 
Lecturer: ____________________ 
Date Issued: ____________________ 
Submission Date: ____________________ 
Total Marks: 100 

INSTRUCTIONS 
1. This is an assessed Computer Science assignment. 
2. The assignment consists of two main parts: 
➢ Part A: Theoretical Foundations (Written Notes) 
➢ Part B: Integrated Practical Implementation 
3. All answers must be typed and professionally formatted. 
4. Clearly structure your work using headings and subheadings. 
5. All code must be written in Python. 
6. Submit: 
➢ A PDF report (theory + explanations + analysis). 
➢ A Jupyter Notebook (.ipynb) or Python script (.py). 
7. Code must be executable and reproducible. 
8. Academic integrity rules apply. Plagiarism will result in penalties.

PART A: THEORETICAL FOUNDATIONS (30 MARKS) 
Students are required to prepare structured academic notes on the topics below. Each 
section must include: 
• 
Definitions 
• 
Clear explanations 
• 
Examples 
• 
Diagrams where appropriate 
• 
Short illustrative code snippets (where relevant) 
• 
Discussion of limitations or challenges 

SECTION 1: TEXT PREPROCESSING (10 Marks) 
1.1 Tokenization 
Explain: 
• 
Sentence segmentation 
• 
Word tokenization 
• 
Challenges in tokenization (punctuation, contractions, multi-word expressions, 
hyphenation) 
• 
Rule-based vs library-based tokenization (e.g., NLTK, spaCy) 
• 
Importance of tokenization in downstream NLP tasks 

1.2 Normalization 
Explain: 
• 
Lowercasing 
• 
Removing punctuation 
• 
Stopword removal 
• 
Noise handling 
• 
Use of regular expressions in text cleaning

Discuss when normalization may negatively affect certain NLP tasks. 

1.3 Stemming and Lemmatization 
Explain: 
• 
Porter Stemmer 
• 
Snowball Stemmer 
• 
WordNet Lemmatizer 
• 
Differences between heuristic stemming and linguistic lemmatization 
• 
Examples showing overstemming and correct lemmatization 

1.4 WordNet and Lexical Resources 
Explain: 
• 
Structure of WordNet 
• 
Synsets 
• 
Hypernyms and hyponyms 
• 
Antonyms 
• 
Role of lexical databases in semantic analysis 

1.5 Frequency Analysis 
Explain: 
• 
Word frequency distribution 
• 
Type-token ratio 
• 
Vocabulary richness 
• 
Zipf’s Law 
• 
Interpretation of frequency statistics in corpus analysis

SECTION 2: MORPHOLOGY (10 Marks) 
2.1 Morphological Structure 
Explain: 
• 
Morphemes 
• 
Roots, prefixes, suffixes 
• 
Inflectional vs derivational morphology 
• 
Provide examples in English and one additional language 

2.2 Finite-State Morphological Analysis 
Explain: 
• 
Finite-State Automata (FSA) 
• 
Finite-State Transducers (FST) 
• 
Surface form vs lexical representation 
• 
Why FSTs are suitable for modeling morphology 
Provide a conceptual diagram illustrating plural formation using an FST. 

2.3 Stemming vs Full Morphological Analysis 
Discuss: 
• 
Limitations of stemming 
• 
Advantages of morphological analyzers 
• 
Handling irregular forms 

2.4 Compounds and Clitics 
Explain: 
• 
Compound word formation 
• 
Tokenization ambiguity

• 
Contractions and clitics 
• 
Why defining a “word” is non-trivial in NLP 

SECTION 3: LANGUAGE MODELING WITH N-GRAMS (5 Marks) 
Explain: 
• 
Definition of a language model 
• 
Unigram, bigram, trigram models 
• 
Markov assumption 
• 
Maximum likelihood estimation 
• 
Smoothing (Laplace) 
• 
Perplexity 
• 
Limitations of N-gram models 
Include at least one worked probability example. 

SECTION 4: PART-OF-SPEECH TAGGING (5 Marks) 
Explain: 
• 
POS tagging task 
• 
Penn Treebank tagset (with examples) 
• 
Rule-based vs statistical tagging 
• 
Hidden Markov Model formulation 
• 
Emission and transition probabilities 
• 
Ambiguity in tagging 
• 
Sequence labeling concept 

PART B: INTEGRATED PRACTICAL IMPLEMENTATION (70 MARKS) 
TITLE:

Development of a Mini Text Analytics and Language Modeling System 
You are required to design and implement a complete NLP processing pipeline using 
Python. 
You may use: 
• 
NLTK 
• 
spaCy 
• 
Standard Python libraries 

TASK REQUIREMENTS 
You will be provided with a raw English text corpus (or you may use an NLTK corpus such as 
Brown or Reuters with permission). 

TASK 1: TEXT PREPROCESSING (15 Marks) 
Perform: 
1. Sentence tokenization 
2. Word tokenization 
3. Normalization (case folding, punctuation removal, stopword removal) 
4. Comparison between stemming and lemmatization 
Compute and report: 
• 
Vocabulary size before cleaning 
• 
Vocabulary size after cleaning 
• 
Type-token ratio 
• 
Top 20 most frequent words 
Provide analysis of the impact of preprocessing. 

TASK 2: MORPHOLOGICAL ANALYSIS (10 Marks) 
1. Manually analyze 15 words from the corpus into morphemes.

2. Implement a simple rule-based affix stripping function. 
3. Compare results with: 
o Porter Stemmer 
o Snowball Stemmer 
4. Use spaCy (if available) to inspect morphological features such as tense and 
number. 
Discuss limitations observed. 

TASK 3: WORDNET LEXICAL ANALYSIS (10 Marks) 
1. Select 5 frequent words. 
2. Extract: 
➢ Synsets 
➢ Hypernyms 
➢ Example synonyms 
3. Demonstrate how synonym expansion could improve search or retrieval. 

TASK 4: BUILDING AN N-GRAM LANGUAGE MODEL (15 Marks) 
1. Compute unigram frequencies. 
2. Compute bigram frequencies. 
3. Estimate conditional probabilities. 
4. Implement Laplace smoothing. 
5. Calculate probability of a given test sentence. 
6. Compute approximate perplexity. 

TASK 5: TEXT GENERATION (5 Marks) 
Using your bigram model:

1. Generate at least 5 random sentences. 
2. Compare output with and without smoothing. 
3. Discuss coherence and limitations. 

TASK 6: POS TAGGING AND EVALUATION (10 Marks) 
1. Use NLTK to tag the corpus. 
2. Train a unigram tagger. 
3. Compare: 
➢ Default tagger accuracy 
➢ Unigram tagger accuracy 
4. Identify at least 10 tagging errors. 
5. Provide linguistic explanations for the errors. 

TASK 7: CRITICAL REFLECTION (5 Marks) 
Answer the following: 
1. How does preprocessing influence language modeling results? 
2. How does morphology affect vocabulary size and sparsity? 
3. Why is smoothing necessary? 
4. What limitations did you observe in N-gram models? 
5. How might neural language models improve performance?

MARKING SCHEME 
Component 
Marks 
Part A – Theory 30 
Preprocessing 15 
Morphology 
10 
WordNet 
10 
N-gram Model 15 
Text Generation 5 
POS Tagging 
10 
Reflection 
5 
Total 
100 

EXPECTED LEARNING OUTCOMES 
Upon completion of this assignment, students should be able to: 
1. Design and implement a structured NLP preprocessing pipeline. 
2. Analyze morphological structure computationally and linguistically. 
3. Construct and evaluate statistical language models. 
4. Implement and assess POS tagging systems. 
5. Critically evaluate limitations of classical NLP methods. 

End of Assignment
